name: Yacumama
description: Yacumama is a serpent from Peru folklore. Massive serpent spirit ruling rivers and lakes, controlling aquatic life and punishing those who disrespect waters. Amazonian water spirit. Represents respect for waterways. It is said to dwell in Rivers, lakes, Amazon.

behavior: Yacumama is strangling, poisonous, territorial in Peru folklore.

seo_description: Massive serpent spirit ruling rivers and lakes, controlling aquatic life and punishing those who disrespect waters.
source: Amazonian folklore; the Yacumama is the mother of all water and a giant serpent.

name: Yagwanggwi
description: Yagwanggwi is a household-spirit from South Korea folklore. Shoe-Stealing Ghost descending from heavens on Lunar New Year's Eve. Wanders stealing shoes - shoes represent destiny and luck. Korean New Year tradition. Shoes represent Palja (destiny/luck). Unique Korean ghost tradition. It is said to dwell in Villages, outside homes.

name: Yaksha
description: Yaksha is a nature-spirit from India folklore. Nature spirits and guardians of treasure. Attendants of Kubera, god of wealth. Inhabit trees, forests, places of natural beauty. Represents sacred relationship between humans and nature. Connected to fertility and abundance. It is said to dwell in Forests, sacred trees, mountains, treasure hoard.

name: Yakshini
description: Female nature spirits in Hindu, Buddhist, and Jain mythology, attendants of Kubera (god of wealth). Associated with the ashoka and sal trees, guarding hidden treasures of earth. Can be benevolent guardians or malevolent poltergeist-like spirits that haunt and curse humans.
appearance: Yakshini takes the form of a forest-spirit known in India folklore.
behavior: Benevolent yakshinis protect trees and grant prosperity. Malicious ones can cause illness and misfortune. Associated with fertility - the young woman at the foot of the tree is an ancient motif. Respond to devotees in Tantric traditions.
habitat: Sacred groves, banyan and peepal trees, Shiva temples, water bodies
cultural_significance: Important in early Buddhist monuments as decorative elements - became Salabhanjikas over centuries. Worshipped as tutelaries. Thirty-six yakshinis described in Uddamareshvara Tantra with specific mantras. Guardians of treasure hidden in earth.
summary: Female nature spirits in Hindu, Buddhist, and Jain mythology, attendants of Kubera (god of wealth)
fun_fact: Important in early Buddhist monuments as decorative elements - became Salabhanjikas over centuries. Worshipped as tutelaries. Thirty-six yakshinis described in Uddamareshvara Tantra with specific mantras. Guardians of treasure hidden in earth.
seo_description: Female nature spirits in Hindu, Buddhist, and Jain mythology, attendants of Kubera (god of wealth). Associated with the ashoka and sal trees, guarding
source: India mythology, oral tradition & folklore collections

Live in deep waterholes and rockholes, can drag people underwater and drown them. Kunwinjku water spirit. Represents dangerous water forces in Arnhem Land. It is said to dwell in Deep waterholes, rockholes.

name: Yowie
description: A large, hairy, ape-like creature from Australian Aboriginal oral tradition, often compared to Bigfoot. Known by many names across language groups including Yahoo, Joogabinna, Barga, Dulagal, Pangkarlangu.
appearance: Large bipedal creature, 6-10 feet tall, covered in thick shaggy fur (black, brown, or gray). Powerful chest and shoulders, long arms, humanoid but not human. Prominent brow ridge, deep-set eyes, sometimes with fangs or tusks.
behavior: Yowie is nocturnal, protect, guardian, guard, dangerous in Australia folklore.
habitat: Dense forests, bushland, mountainous regions of eastern Australia
cultural_significance: Deep roots in Aboriginal Dreamtime stories as powerful non-human beings that enforce cultural law. Warnings for children not to wander alone. Connection to boundary between human and spirit worlds.
summary: A large, hairy, ape-like creature from Australian Aboriginal oral tradition, often compared to Bigfoot

seo_description: A large, hairy, ape-like creature from Australian Aboriginal oral tradition, often compared to Bigfoot. Known by many names across language groups inc

description: Yuki-onna is a ghost from Japan folklore. A snow woman who appears during blizzards, freezing travelers to death. Sometimes falls in love with humans and can be gentle. Personification of winter's deadly beauty. Represents emotional coldness and unrequited love. It is said to dwell in Snowy mountains, winter storms.

Each has different naughty habit like door-peeping, candle-stealing. Icelandic Christmas tradition. Children leave shoes, receive gifts or potatoes. It is said to dwell in Homes during Christmas season.

name: Yamm
description: Yamm is the Canaanite-Phoenician god of the sea, rivers, and primordial chaos. As the deity representing the untamed and destructive power of the Mediterranean, Yamm was especially significant to the maritime Phoenicians of Lebanon's coastal cities. Depicted as consistently tyrannical and angry, he was the brother of Mot and the son of El, the supreme god. His great myth is told in the Ugaritic Baal Cycle where he demands tribute from all the gods and claims dominion over them. Only Baal Hadad, the storm god, dares to oppose him. Armed with two divine maces forged by Kothar-wa-Khasis, Baal fights and defeats Yamm, scattering his body across the sea. This Chaoskampf myth directly inspired the biblical accounts of Yahweh defeating Leviathan.
appearance: Yamm is depicted as a massive, serpentine sea dragon or multi-headed chaos monster arising from the waters, described as a great serpent or dragon with seven heads, covered in scales, with gaping jaws.
behavior: A tyrannical and destructive force who demands absolute submission from gods and mortals alike, representing the uncontrollable aspects of the sea—storms, shipwrecks, and coastal flooding.
habitat: The Mediterranean Sea, the abyss, the depths of the ocean
strengths: Control over the sea and all waters
cultural_significance: Yamm is the most direct Canaanite precursor to the biblical Leviathan, whose Chaoskampf myth influenced Jewish, Christian, and Islamic apocalyptic literature.
summary: The Canaanite-Phoenician god of the sea and chaos, defeated by Baal in the Ugaritic Baal Cycle, precursor to the biblical Leviathan
fun_fact: Yamm was the Canaanite-Phoenician god of the sea and chaos, defeated by Baal in the Baal Cycle—direct precursor to the biblical Leviathan.
keywords: Yamm, Canaanite sea god, Phoenician mythology, Baal Cycle, Chaoskampf, Leviathan precursor
search_terms: Yamm Canaanite god, Baal vs Yamm, Ugaritic sea monster
seo_title: Yamm: The Canaanite-Phoenician God of the Sea and Chaos
seo_description: Yamm was the Canaanite-Phoenician god of the sea and chaos, defeated by Baal in the Baal Cycle—direct precursor to the biblical Leviathan.
source: World History Encyclopedia, academic publications on Ugaritic and Canaanite religion

source_references.t: Yamm: Sea God of Phoenician Mythology

name: Yam-Nahar
description: Yam-Nahar (literally 'Sea-River') is the god of the sea and rivers in ancient Syrian (Ugaritic) mythology, the personification of the destructive power of the waters. Known as Prince Yam and Judge Nahar, he is son of the supreme god El and the beloved of his father. Yam is depicted as a serpentine deity, often identified with the sea monster Lotan (Leviathan). In the Baal Cycle, Yam challenges the storm god Baal for kingship of the gods, sending messengers to demand Baal's tribute. Baal defeats Yam in cosmic combat using divine weapons crafted by the craftsman god Kothar-wa-Khasis, establishing order over chaos. This Chaoskampf myth is one of the earliest recorded divine combat narratives and directly influenced the Babylonian Enuma Elish, Hebrew Bible imagery of Yahweh's defeat of Leviathan, and Greek myths of Zeus versus Typhon.
appearance: Ophidian (snake-like) deity associated with the sea. Can appear as a churning sea, a flooding river, or a massive serpent. Crowded with waves personified as his army.
behavior: Demands tribute and submission from other gods. Aggressive and powerful. Seeks kingship. Represents the untamed destructive force of the sea.
habitat: The cosmic sea, the deep, the abyss — waters of chaos
weaknesses: vulnerable to divine weapons of Kothar
cultural_significance: One of the most important deities of the Ugaritic pantheon. The Baal-Yam conflict is the foundational Chaoskampf (struggle against chaos) narrative of the Levant. Yam's defeat established the pattern for divine combat myths from Mesopotamia to Greece and the Hebrew Bible.
summary: Canaanite sea god from ancient Syrian mythology who personifies the destructive power of the sea and battles the storm god Baal for kingship
fun_fact: Yam's epithet 'Judge River' (tapit nahr) may refer to a cosmic river that served as a boundary between the living and the dead, making Yam both a sea god and a figure connected to the underworld.
seo_title: Yam-Nahar – The Primordial Sea God of Ancient Syrian (Ugaritic) Myth
seo_description: Yam-Nahar is the Canaanite god of the sea from ancient Syrian mythology who battles Baal for divine kingship in the foundational Chaoskampf myth.
source: KTU 1.1-1.6 (Ugaritic Baal Cycle); scholarly works by Mark Smith, Jürg Eggler, John Day

name: Yule Cat
description: A colossal and vicious feline that prowls the snowy Icelandic countryside during the Christmas season. It is said to devour anyone who has not received a new piece of clothing before Christmas Eve, serving as a folk incentive for farm workers to finish their wool production.

behavior: Yule Cat is predatory, punishing, nocturnal in Iceland folklore.
habitat: Snowy countryside, mountains, and frozen villages
cultural_significance: Traditional Icelandic incentive for diligence in wool production and a staple of Christmas folklore.
summary: A colossal and vicious feline that devours those who do not receive new clothes before Christmas
fun_fact: The Yule Cat is a legendary Icelandic monster that eats those who do not receive new clothes for Christmas.
seo_description: The Yule Cat is a legendary Icelandic monster that eats those who do not receive new clothes for Christmas.
source: Icelandic folklore, Jón Árnason's collections
source_references: Árni Björnsson, Jól og áramót (1995); Icelandic folklore documentation

name: Yatoka (Sun Father)
description: Yatoka is the Sun Father in Zuni mythology, the form taken by Áwonawílona (the All-Father) when he made himself into the sun. The mist clouds of the Sun Father formed into the Great Waters from which Earth Mother and Sky Father emerged. Yatoka is the father of the Twin Children of the Sun (the Ahayuta twins). He is the source of life, light, and warmth and receives daily prayers and offerings from the Zuni people. The Sun Father is central to Zuni agricultural and ceremonial life.
appearance: Yatoka is the Sun Father in Zuni mythology, the form taken by Áwonawílona (the All-Father) when he made himself into the sun. The mist clouds of the Sun Father formed into the Great Waters from which Earth Mother and Sky Father emerged.
behavior: Described in Zuni oral traditions and ceremonial narratives.
habitat: Southwest United States, ancestral Zuni lands
cultural_significance: Featured in the oral traditions and ceremonial practices of the Zuni people.

source: Zuni mythology; Yatoka is the Sun Father, a form of Áwonawílona.

name: Yanauluha (Culture Hero)
description: Yanauluha is a benevolent culture hero in Zuni mythology who brought the arts of medicine and agriculture to the people. After the emergence into the daylight world, he appeared carrying seeds, digging sticks, and sacred objects. He taught which plants were edible and medicinal, how to plant corn and other crops, and how to observe celestial cycles for planting timing. He also taught how to use plants for healing. Yanauluha established the pattern of the agricultural year and the accompanying ce.
appearance: After the emergence into the daylight world, he appeared carrying seeds, digging sticks, and sacred objects.
behavior: Described in Zuni oral traditions and ceremonial narratives.
habitat: Southwest United States, ancestral Zuni lands
cultural_significance: Featured in the oral traditions and ceremonial practices of the Zuni people.
summary: Yanauluha is a benevolent culture hero in Zuni mythology who brought the arts of medicine and agriculture to the people. After the emergence into the daylight world, he appeared carrying seeds, diggin
fun_fact: Featured in the oral traditions and ceremonial practices of the Zuni people.
seo_description: Yanauluha is a benevolent culture hero in Zuni mythology who brought the arts of medicine and agriculture to the people. After the emergence into the daylight w
source: Zuni mythology; Yanauluha is a culture hero who brought arts to the people.

source_references.t: Yanauluha: Culture Hero of the Zuni

name: Yunwi Tsunsdi
description: Yunwi Tsunsdi are the Cherokee Little People — small, child-sized nature spirits who live in caves, under rocks, and in the dense forest. They are usually invisible but sometimes reveal themselves to humans they trust. They possess powerful magic and are said to have taught medicine to certain shamans.
appearance: Beautiful little people standing knee-high with perfect miniature Cherokee features and long hair reaching their waists, dressed in tiny traditional clothing. This spirit appears in the traditional fo
behavior: Cherokee little people living in rock shelters who help lost children and punish those who disrespect nature, granting good luck or causing mischief depending on treatment. In traditional stories and
habitat: Caves, under rocks, and dense forests of Cherokee territory
cultural_significance: This being is an important figure in Cherokee mythology and spiritual tradition, representing the rich oral literature of the Cherokee people as documented by ethnographers like James Mooney.
summary: The Yunwi Tsunsdi is yunwi tsunsdi are the cherokee little people — small, child-sized nature spirits who live in caves, under rocks, and in the dense forest. they are usually invisible but sometimes reveal themselves to
fun_fact: Cherokee tradition holds that speaking directly about encounters with the Little People can bring bad luck
seo_description: The Yunwi Tsunsdi is a legendary being from Cherokee mythology. Yunwi Tsunsdi are the Cherokee Little People — small, child-sized nature spirits who live in cav
source: Wikipedia - Cherokee spiritual beliefs; native-languages.org Yunwi Tsunsdi

name: Yeil (Raven)
description: Yeil is the Tlingit name for Raven, the central trickster and culture hero of Tlingit mythology. Yeil is responsible for many fundamental features of the world. He created the tides by making a woman under the earth to control the rise and fall of water. In the Tlingit flood narrative, Raven commanded the tide woman to raise the water. When Raven returned, he turned the remaining people and animals into stone and made new human beings from leaves, explaining why people die in autumn when leaves fall. Yeil is both creator and destroyer.
appearance: Yeil appears as a large, glossy black raven with intelligent eyes, capable of transforming into human form as a wiry clever-looking man with black hair and sharp features.
behavior: Yeil is endlessly curious, scheming, and hungry. He creates through destruction and teaches through negative example. His selfish actions paradoxically bring benefits to humanity.
habitat: All of Southeast Alaska, from glaciers to islands, particularly the Nass River and Inside Passage.
cultural_significance: Yeil is the most important figure in Tlingit mythology. The Raven moiety traces its origin to Yeil. His stories teach proper behavior and explain natural phenomena.
summary: Yeil (Raven) is the central trickster and culture hero of Tlingit mythology who created the tides and shaped the landscape.
fun_fact: Yeil is the most important figure in Tlingit mythology. The Raven moiety traces its origin to Yeil. His stories teach proper behavior and explain natural phenomena.
source: John R. Swanton, Tlingit Myths and Texts (1909)
source_references: Tlingit folklore documentation; raven creator studies

name: Yulm (First Carpenter)
description: Yulm is the eldest of the Four Carpenters (Masmasalanicw) in Nuxalk mythology. He created Raven from a piece of charcoal by shaking it in his hands. When he opened his hands, it was a bird. The other carpenters gave Raven wings, eyes, and life. Yulm represents the first and most senior of the creative intermediaries who shaped the world under Alhkw'ntam direction.
appearance: Yulm is the eldest Carpenter, a wise and skilled being. He creates Raven from charcoal through a simple but powerful act.
behavior: Yulm creates Raven from charcoal, then the other carpenters complete the work. He is the leader of the Four Carpenters.
habitat: The House of Smayusta in the upper world.
cultural_significance: Yulm is the first among the Four Carpenters, credited with creating Raven. His story is part of the Nuxalk creation cycle.
summary: Yulm is the eldest of the Four Carpenters in Nuxalk mythology who created Raven from a piece of charcoal.
fun_fact: Yulm is the first among the Four Carpenters, credited with creating Raven. His story is part of the Nuxalk creation cycle.
source: Franz Boas, The Mythology of the Bella Coola Indians (1898)

name: Yurugu (Pale Fox)
description: Yurugu (also called Ogo, the Pale Fox) is the first being of Dogon creation, born prematurely from Amma's flawed creative act. He limps across the Sahel on uneven legs as the living embodiment of disorder and incompleteness. Despite his chaotic nature, Yurugu serves a vital purpose through divination: each night, Dogon diviners draw a rectangular grid in fine sand, and Yurugu walks it, leaving paw-prints that are read at dawn to interpret the future. Because he is the original wound of creation, his tracks reveal truth. This paradox lies at the heart of Dogon spirituality: disorder, read correctly, becomes a map of truth.
appearance: Pale fox with uneven legs, giving him a distinctive limp. Reddish-tan fur, sharp features. Sometimes described as humanoid with fox-like characteristics. Moves at night.
behavior: Trickster who introduced chaos into creation. Wanders at night, walking divination grids. Unpredictable, limping gait. Truth-teller despite his disruptive nature.
habitat: The Sahel, the Bandiagara Escarpment, the edges of Dogon villages (where divination grids are drawn)
diet: Kola nuts (from divination offerings), small game
cultural_impact: The Pale Fox divination system is one of the most complex in West Africa, integrated into every major decision in Dogon communities from planting to marriage.
symbolism: The original wound of creation; incompleteness as a source of knowledge; the trickster as truth-bearer
fun_fact: Yurugu's uneven legs symbolize cosmic imbalance: he is said to have stolen seeds from Amma and introduced chaos because he was born without his female twin, an incompleteness that drives all his actions.
summary: Dogon pale fox trickster who introduced chaos into creation and reveals truth through divination tracks
source: Marcel Griaule & Germaine Dieterlen, The Pale Fox (1965)
cultural_significance: Yurugu (Pale Fox) is a a trickster from West Africa mythology, representing important cultural values and traditional beliefs.

name: Yemoja
description: Yemoja is the great mother goddess of the Yoruba pantheon, the orisha of the ocean, motherhood, fertility, and the Ogun River. Her name means "Mother whose children are the fish." She is considered the mother of many orishas and the protective spirit of women, children, and sailors. As the primordial ocean from which all life emerged, Yemoja represents the maternal essence of the universe. In times of drought, she emerges from the depths carrying the moon in her womb, bringing tides and rains. Her devotees dress in blue and white and offer her rice, fish, and gourds.
appearance: Beautiful, buxom woman emerging from ocean waves. Wears blue and white flowing garments with fish-tail details. Often depicted with a crescent moon and holding a mirror or fan. Can appear as a mermaid.
behavior: Nurturing mother figure who protects women during childbirth. Calms turbulent waters. Brings fertility and abundance. Can be fierce when her children are threatened. Controls the tides and ocean currents.
habitat: Oceans, rivers, especially the Ogun River in Nigeria, seashores, and underwater realms
cultural_significance: Yemoja is revered across the African diaspora as Yemayá in Cuban Santería and Iemanjá in Brazilian Candomblé, where her festival on February 2 draws millions. She is syncretized with the Virgin Mary in Catholic traditions.
summary: Yemoja is the mother of waters and the maternal essence of the Yoruba universe — the great ocean orisha who nurtures all life and protects women and children
fun_fact: Yemoja is the mother of waters and the maternal essence of the Yoruba universe — the great ocean orisha who nurtures all life and protects women and c
source: Yoruba oral tradition, Wikipedia, academic folklore collections
seo_description: Yemoja is the mother of waters and the maternal essence of the Yoruba universe — the great ocean orisha who nurtures all life and protects women and c
source_references: Verger, Dieux Yoruba (1954/1995); Johnson, The History of the Yorubas (1921); Drewal, Sacred Waters (2008)

name: Yewe
description: Yewe (also known as Hevioso, Xevioso, or Sogbo) is the powerful Ewe god of thunder, lightning, rain, and divine justice. One of the major trɔwo (lesser deities descended from Mawu), Yewe is one of the most feared and respected spirits in the Ewe pantheon. His sacred cult, the Yewe society, is one of the most exclusive and highly developed spiritual organizations in Eweland, with elaborate initiation ceremonies involving seclusion, sacred drumming (Sowu), and ritual dances. Those who offend Yewe may be struck by lightning. His priests (Midawos) serve as intermediaries. Yewe's sacred color is red, and his priests dance in possession trance during annual festivals. Yewe is related to the Yoruba Shango and the Fon Xevioso, reflecting the interconnected spiritual traditions of the region.
appearance: Powerful figure wielding a double-headed axe (symbol of thunder). Clothed in red. Surrounded by lightning. Carries thunderstones. His priests wear red and white in ceremonies.
behavior: God of thunder who strikes with lightning. Dispenses divine justice. Requires proper initiation for his cult. Possesses priests during ceremonial drumming. Demands moral conduct from followers.
habitat: The sky, thunderclouds, sacred groves, Yewe cult houses in Ewe communities
cultural_significance: The Yewe cult is one of the most important traditional religious societies in Eweland, with highly developed sacred music systems (Sowu drumming). The cult maintains strict moral standards and provides community education. Yewe initiation creates powerful social bonds.
summary: Yewe is the Ewe god of thunder and lightning — the fearsome deity whose exclusive cult society commands the storms and enforces divine justice.
fun_fact: Yewe is the Ewe god of thunder and lightning — the fearsome deity whose exclusive cult society commands the storms and enforces divine justice.
source: Ewe religion of Togo — Yewe thunder god in the Ewe pantheon.
seo_description: Yewe is the Ewe god of thunder and lightning — the fearsome deity whose exclusive cult society commands the storms and enforces divine justice.
source_references: Westermann, Die Ewe-Sprache (1931, Reimer); Spieth, Die Ewe-Stämme (1906, Dietrich Reimer); Rouget, La musique et la transe (1980, Gallimard)

name: Saint Yared
description: Saint Yared is the legendary 6th-century Ethiopian saint who founded the liturgical music tradition of the Ethiopian Orthodox Tewahedo Church. Born in Axum, Yared was a slow learner as a child who often ran away from his studies. One day, inspired by watching a caterpillar persistently climb a stalk after repeated falls, he returned to his studies with renewed dedication. He was later taken to Heaven where three Holy Spirits taught him the arts of vocal performance, composition, and poetry. He created the five-note scale system (zema) and composed the Deggua (hymn book), inventing musical notation for the Ge'ez language. His music was so beautiful that angels descended from heaven to listen. He served Emperor Gebre Meskel until retiring to the Semien mountains where he is said to have been taken up to heaven without dying.
appearance: Depicted in Ethiopian iconography as a bearded saint in priestly vestments, holding a sistrum (tsenatsel) and prayer staff, often with three birds or angels nearby.
behavior: Devout composer and hymnist who channels divine inspiration into sacred music. Ascetic lifestyle dedicated to prayer and meditation. Patient teacher who gathers disciples.
habitat: Axum, Semien mountains, Ethiopian Orthodox monasteries, Church of Mary of Zion
cultural_significance: Saint Yared is considered the father of Ethiopian music and liturgical tradition. His three modes (Ge'ez, Ezil, Araray) remain the foundation of Ethiopian Orthodox worship.
summary: Saint Yared is the 6th-century Ethiopian saint and musical genius who created the five-note scale system and composed the Deggua, founding Ethiopian Orthodox liturgical music.
fun_fact: Saint Yared is the 6th-century Ethiopian saint and musical genius who created the five-note scale system and composed the Deggua, founding Ethiopian Orthodox liturgical music.
source: Ethiopian Orthodox tradition — Saint Yared is the father of Ethiopian music.
seo_description: Saint Yared is the 6th-century Ethiopian saint and musical genius who created the five-note scale system and composed the Deggua, founding Ethiopian Orthodox liturgical music.

name: Yara-ma-yha-who
description: The Yara-ma-yha-who is a vampiric spirit from Aboriginal Australian Dreaming legends, particularly in the mythology of the Yuin people of southeastern Australia. This creature is described as a small, red-skinned being standing about four feet tall with an oversized head, a large mouth, and long, skinny limbs. Each of its hands and feet ends in suckers like those of an octopus or frog. The Yara-ma-yha-who lives in fig trees and drops down on unsuspecting travelers, using its suckers to drain their blood. After feeding, the creature swells up and then drops the victim, who wakes up weaker but alive. The process repeats each time the victim passes beneath the tree, and with each encounter the victim grows shorter and redder until eventually becoming a Yara-ma-yha-who themselves.
appearance: Small humanoid with red skin standing about four feet tall. Oversized head and mouth. Long skinny limbs with sucker-tipped fingers and toes like an octopus. Swells after feeding.
behavior: Vampiric ambush predator that drops from fig trees onto travelers. Drains blood through suckers. Repeated feeding transforms victims into the same creature. Preys on solitary bush travelers.
habitat: Fig trees in the forests of southeastern Australia
weaknesses: swells and becomes slow after feeding
cultural_significance: The Yara-ma-yha-who legend reinforces the dangers of traveling alone through unfamiliar bush territory and serves as a moral warning about the consequences of foolish behavior.
summary: Vampiric spirit from Aboriginal Dreaming legends that drops from fig trees and drains blood through sucker-covered hands and feet
fun_fact: Vampiric spirit from Aboriginal Dreaming legends that drops from fig trees and drains blood through sucker-covered hands and feet
search_terms: yara ma yha who aboriginal vampire spirit
source: Australian Aboriginal folklore — the Yara-ma-yha-who is a vampiric cryptid from southeastern Australian Aboriginal tradition.
seo_description: Vampiric spirit from Aboriginal Dreaming legends that drops from fig trees and drains blood through sucker-covered hands and feet
source_references: Tindale, aboriginal folklore collections; Berndt & Berndt, The Speaking Land (1989)

name: Yuch-Kurbustan
description: Supreme divine figure in Altai mythology, representing the highest celestial authority. Yuch-Kurbustan is invoked in epic narratives as the ultimate source of divine power and justice. In the Maadai-Kara epic, Yuch-Kurbustan provides aid to the hero Kögüdei Mergen in his quest against the dark forces of Erlik.
appearance: Celestial being of pure light residing in the highest heavens. Not depicted in human form — represented as pure divine radiance.
behavior: Supreme divine authority. Provides divine aid to heroes. Judges the actions of gods and humans alike.
habitat: Highest level of the celestial realm
cultural_significance: Represents the highest level of divine hierarchy in Altai cosmology. Invoked in the most sacred epic narratives.
summary: Yuch-Kurbustan is a deity from Altai folklore in Russia, believed to embody the spiritual traditions of the region.
fun_fact: In the Maadai-Kara epic, Yuch-Kurbustan aids the hero Kögüdei Mergen in his quest against Erlik's forces.
seo_description: Supreme divine figure in Altai mythology, representing the highest celestial authority. Yuch-Kurbustan is invoked in epic narratives as the ultimate source of d
source: Altai oral tradition; Maadai-Kara epic

name: Yul-Chyn (Ice Spirits)
description: Malevolent ice spirits of Nenets mythology born from the frozen breath of the primordial mammoth. Yul-Chyn dwell within the permafrost of the Yamal Peninsula, emerging during the ferocious Arctic winter to freeze travelers, crack the earth, and torment those caught in blizzards. They are the living embodiment of the lethal cold that threatens all life on the tundra.
appearance: Translucent, shimmering forms of frost and ice that can appear as swirling columns of frozen air, skeletal figures made of icicles, or formless cold mists.
behavior: Cruel and predatory; they freeze exposed skin instantly, lead travelers astray in whiteouts, and creep into dwellings through cracks. They weaken as the sun strengthens.
habitat: Yamal Peninsula permafrost, Arctic tundra
cultural_significance: Personify the lethal Arctic cold that defines life on the Yamal Peninsula; offerings are made before winter journeys to appease them.
summary: Malevolent ice spirits of Nenets folklore, embodiments of deadly Arctic cold
fun_fact: Personify the lethal Arctic cold that defines life on the Yamal Peninsula; offerings are made before winter journeys to appease them.
seo_description: Malevolent ice spirits of Nenets folklore, embodiments of deadly Arctic cold
source: Nenets indigenous tradition — spirits of the Nenets people.

name: Yeti (Migoi)
description: Powerful apelike creature of Tibetan Himalayan folklore, known as Migoi ('wild man'). In Tibetan tradition the Yeti is not merely a physical cryptid but a spiritual guardian of the high mountains, often associated with mountain gods like Nyenchen Thanglha. Some traditions describe it as flesh-and-blood, others as a supernatural being that can become invisible.
appearance: Massive bipedal ape-like creature covered in thick reddish-brown or gray fur, standing 6-10 feet tall. Leaves large footprints in snow. Can reportedly become invisible.
behavior: Shy and reclusive, avoids humans but can be dangerous when threatened. Guardians of sacred mountain passes must be appeased with offerings.
habitat: High Himalayas, remote mountain passes of Tibet
cultural_significance: Living part of Tibetan Himalayan folklore with deep spiritual dimensions; distinct from Western Abominable Snowman depictions.
summary: Powerful apelike guardian spirit of the Himalayas in Tibetan folklore
fun_fact: Living part of Tibetan Himalayan folklore with deep spiritual dimensions; distinct from Western Abominable Snowman depictions.
seo_description: Powerful apelike guardian spirit of the Himalayas in Tibetan folklore
source: Tibetan Himalayan cryptozoology — the Yeti/Meh-Teh cryptid.

name: Yeshe Tsogyal
description: The principal consort of Padmasambhava (Guru Rinpoche) and a fully realized Buddha in her own right. Yeshe Tsogyal concealed countless terma (hidden treasures) throughout Tibet together with Padmasambhava to be discovered by future tertons. She is the female wisdom embodiment of enlightenment, revered as the 'Mother of Tibetan Buddhism.'
appearance: Beautiful luminous female figure with peaceful yet penetrating gaze. Holds a vajra (thunderbolt scepter) and skull cup, seated or standing in dancing pose.
behavior: Wisdom incarnate; perfectly realized female Buddha who actively teaches, conceals terma, and guides practitioners. Embodies the feminine principle of enlightenment.
habitat: Tibet, hidden caves, sacred sites
cultural_significance: The most revered female figure in Tibetan Buddhism, embodying the possibility of full enlightenment in a female body.
summary: Principal female Buddha of Tibetan Buddhism, consort of Padmasambhava
fun_fact: Principal female Buddha of Tibetan Buddhism, consort of Padmasambhava
seo_description: Principal female Buddha of Tibetan Buddhism, consort of Padmasambhava
source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Yeshe_Tsogyal

source_references.t: Yeshe Tsogyal: Mother of Tibetan Buddhism

name: Yawm Saub (God of Creation)
description: Yawm Saub (also Yer Shau or simply Saub — 'The One Who Knows Everything') is the high god and creator in Hmong cosmology. He is the supreme celestial being who created the world and all living things, though he is somewhat remote and delegates daily affairs to lesser spirits. Saub is depicted as polygynous, living in a celestial house with a garden and pigs — very human in his nature yet all-seeing and all-knowing. When the evil spirit Ntxwj Nyog began devouring human souls, Saub sent the first shaman Siv Yis to combat him. It was Saub who advised the surviving siblings after the great flood to marry and repopulate the earth, and who told them to carve the resulting mass into twelve pieces that became the Hmong clans. Saub provided the first seeds for agriculture, caused the first hen to lay eggs, and taught humanity proper rituals. Unlike Ntxwj Nyog who is actively involved in causing harm, Saub is benevolent but distant — he set the world in motion and provides shamans with power but rarely intervenes directly.
appearance: An old, wise man with long white hair and beard, dressed in white robes, living in a celestial house with a garden. Sometimes depicted as pure radiance. Has a gentle, knowing expression. Carries a staff or a book of wisdom.
behavior: Wise, benevolent, and patient. Created the world but takes a hands-off approach to its daily running. Answers prayers through the shamanic system. Values merit and virtue. Can appear in human form to guide worthy individuals.
habitat: The upper realm (upper yeeb ceeb), a celestial house with a garden in heaven
cultural_significance: The supreme deity in Hmong folk religion. Though remote, Saub's creation of the shamanic system is the foundation of Hmong spiritual practice. He represents ultimate cosmic authority and moral order.
summary: Supreme creator god in Hmong cosmology who sent the first shaman to battle death
fun_fact: Supreme creator god in Hmong cosmology who sent the first shaman to battle death
seo_title: Yawm Saub (God of Creation) – Hmong Folklore
seo_description: Supreme creator god in Hmong cosmology who sent the first shaman to battle death
source: Hmong religion — Yawm Saub supreme creator deity of Hmong cosmology.

name: Yocahu (Taino Supreme God of Fertility)
description: Yocahu (Yucahu Bagua Maorocoti) was the supreme deity and masculine spirit of fertility in Taino mythology -- the greatest of all zemis. His full name, recorded by the Spanish missionary Fray Ramon Pane in 1498, carries multiple meanings: Yucahu means giver of cassava (the staple crop), Bagua means master of the sea, and Maorocoti means conceived without male intervention -- born solely from his mother Atabey, the goddess of fresh water. Yocahu is the supreme creator god who shaped the Earth, created the sun and moon from a cave, and molded the first humans from clay. He is the god of agriculture, peace, and tranquility, representing goodness and order in the Taino universe. His throne is atop El Yunque Peak in Puerto Rico El Yunque National Forest -- the tallest mountain in the reserve, perpetually shrouded in cloud (the Taino called it yuke, meaning White Lands). From there, Yocahu battles the destructive goddess Guabancex, shielding his people from hurricanes. He is also known as the Sleeping Giant (El Gigante Dormido), whose reclining form is visible in the mountain profile.
appearance: Not depicted in a fixed human form. Associated with the summit of El Yunque Peak, the ceiba tree, cassava plants, and zemi stones. Described as a giant draped in green, his form visible in the mountain silhouette. Sometimes represented by three-pointed stone zemis carved with anthropomorphic faces.
behavior: Creator and sustainer of life. Brings fertility to the land, ensures cassava harvests, and provides peace and tranquility. Battles Guabancex to protect his people from hurricanes and chaos. Rules the heavens with Atabey, his mother. Maintains cosmic balance between order and chaos.
habitat: El Yunque Peak (El Yunque National Forest, Puerto Rico), the sky (turei), the heavens, sacred ceiba trees, cassava fields
cultural_significance: Yocahu was the supreme god of the Taino people -- the first Caribbean indigenous group encountered by Columbus. Fray Ramon Pane Account of the Antiquities of the Indians (1498) is the only primary source on Taino mythology, and Yocahu features prominently. After the Spanish conquest, the god name survived in Puerto Rico geography: El Yunque National Forest, the town of Luquillo, and the enduring legend of El Gigante Dormido.
summary: Yocahu was the supreme god of the Taino people, creator of cassava and master of the sea, who ruled from El Yunque Peak in Puerto Rico
fun_fact: Yocahu was the supreme god of the Taino people, creator of cassava and master of the sea, who ruled from El Yunque Peak in Puerto Rico.
seo_title: Yocahu (Taino Supreme God of Fertility) – Puerto Rico Folklore
seo_description: Yocahu was the supreme god of the Taino people, creator of cassava and master of the sea, who ruled from El Yunque Peak in Puerto Rico.
source: Taino oral tradition, Puerto Rico folklore

name: Yule Lads Iceland
description: The Yule Lads (Jólasveinarnir) are thirteen mischievous figures from Icelandic folklore who visit children on the thirteen nights leading up to Christmas. Each has a distinct personality and prank—from stealing food to slamming doors.
appearance: Yule Lads Iceland is known from recorded folklore and oral tradition in Iceland.
behavior: In traditional accounts, Yule Lads Iceland from Europe  plays its role in the mythology and folklore of its culture.
habitat: Not documented.
cultural_significance: Originally depicted as frightening troll-like figures used to discipline children, the Yule Lads were softened in the 20th century into gift-givers. They are accompanied by the child-eating Grýla and the Yule Cat.
summary: The Yule Lads (Jólasveinarnir) are thirteen mischievous figures from Icelandic folklore who visit children on the thirteen nights leading up to Christmas. Each has a distinct personality and prank—from stealing food to slamming doors.
fun_fact: A trickster figure from European mythology.
source: Icelandic Christmas folklore; Jón Árnason's collection (19th century)

name: Yeil Raven Tlingit
description: Yeil (Raven) is the central trickster and creator figure in Tlingit mythology. Raven stole the sun, moon, and stars to bring light to the world, and is responsible for shaping the landscape and bringing salmon to the rivers.
appearance: Yeil Raven Tlingit is known from recorded folklore and oral tradition in United States.
behavior: In traditional accounts, Yeil Raven Tlingit from North America  plays its role in the mythology and folklore of its culture.
habitat: Not documented.
cultural_significance: Raven is the most important figure in Tlingit oral tradition, serving as both creator and trickster. Raven stories explain the origin of natural phenomena and cultural practices. The Raven moiety is one of two primary kinship divisions among the Tlingit.
summary: Yeil (Raven) is the central trickster and creator figure in Tlingit mythology. Raven stole the sun, moon, and stars to bring light to the world, and is responsible for shaping the landscape and bringing salmon to the rivers.
fun_fact: A animal-spirit figure from Indigenous North American mythology.
source: Tlingit oral tradition; ethonographic records

name: Yulm First Carpenter Nuxalk
description: Yulm is the first and greatest carpenter in Nuxalk creation mythology, the architect who shaped the physical world.
appearance: Yulm First Carpenter Nuxalk is a nature-spirit from Canada tradition.
behavior: The Yulm First Carpenter Nuxalk is a nature-spirit from Indigenous North American folklore with documented traditional stories.
habitat: Not documented.
cultural_significance: Yulm is the first and greatest carpenter in Nuxalk creation mythology, the architect who shaped the physical world
summary: Yulm is the first and greatest carpenter in Nuxalk creation mythology, the architect who shaped the physical world.
fun_fact: A nature-spirit figure from Indigenous North American mythology.
source: Nuxalk folklore; documented in Northwest Coast indigenous records.
good

source_references.title: The Nuxalk of Bella Coola: A Guide to Information Sources
source_references.author: John A. Dunn

source_references.title: Myths and Traditions of the Bella Coola Indians
source_references.author: Edward F. Newman

name: Yggdrasil
description: Yggdrasil is the immense cosmic tree at the center of Norse cosmology, connecting the Nine Worlds. Its branches extend into the heavens, and its three roots reach to wellsprings that nourish creation.
appearance: Yggdrasil is known from recorded folklore and oral tradition in Iceland.
behavior: In traditional accounts, Yggdrasil from Europe  plays its role in the mythology and folklore of its culture.
habitat: Not documented.
cultural_significance: Yggdrasil is the axis mundi of the Norse cosmos, described in the Poetic Edda and Prose Edda. It is tended by the Norns who weave fate at its base, and its fate is bound to Ragnarök.
summary: Yggdrasil is the immense cosmic tree at the center of Norse cosmology, connecting the Nine Worlds. Its branches extend into the heavens, and its three roots reach to wellsprings that nourish creation.
fun_fact: A mythical-tree figure from European mythology.
source: Poetic Edda, Prose Edda (Snorri Sturluson, 13th century)
